questions.question:
For integers a, b and c, what would be the minimum and maximum values respectively of a + b + c if log|a| + log|b| + log|c| = 0?

A. -3 and 3
B. -1 and 1
C. -1 and 3
D. 1 and 3
questions.to_find: minimum and maximum values of a + b + c

understand.plain: The condition log|a| + log|b| + log|c| = 0 means log(|a|*|b|*|c|) = 0, so |a|*|b|*|c| = 1. Since a, b, c are integers, each of |a|, |b|, |c| must be a positive integer whose product is 1. The only way three positive integers multiply to 1 is if each equals 1. So each of a, b, c is either +1 or -1. To maximize a+b+c choose all three as +1 giving 3; to minimize choose all three as -1 giving -3.

keywords.explain: log|a| + log|b| + log|c| = 0 collapses to log(|a||b||c|) = 0 by the product rule of logarithms
a, b, c must be integers, so |a|, |b|, |c| are positive integers; the only factorization of 1 into positive integers is 1*1*1
keywords.example: no integer has absolute value between 0 and 1 (exclusive), so each must equal 1

keywords.explain: each variable can independently be +1 or -1, giving sums from -3 to +3
keywords.example: a = -1, b = -1, c = -1 gives sum = -3; all equal +1 gives sum = +3

understand.visual_alt: Three integers whose absolute values multiply to 1 can each only be +1 or -1.

given.aim: Find minimum and maximum of a + b + c subject to |a|*|b|*|c| = 1 with a, b, c integers

terms.meaning: By the product rule, log(|a|*|b|*|c|) = 0, so |a|*|b|*|c| = 1
terms.connects: constrains the absolute values of a, b, c

terms.meaning: a, b, c are whole numbers (no fractions); hence |a|, |b|, |c| >= 1
terms.connects: the only positive integers with product 1 are all equal to 1

terms.meaning: once we know each variable is +1 or -1, the extremes of the sum are found by taking all negative or all positive

given.plan: Convert the log condition to a product condition, use integer constraint to pin |a| = |b| = |c| = 1, then find extreme sums by choosing signs freely.

steps.title: Convert log sum to product condition
steps.note: The base of the logarithm (assumed base 10 or any base > 1) does not affect this argument since log_b(1) = 0 for all valid bases.

steps.title: Determine individual values from the integer constraint
steps.apply: The only factorisation of 1 into positive integers is 1*1*1, so |a| = |b| = |c| = 1, meaning each of a, b, c is either +1 or -1.
steps.note: Any integer has |x| >= 1, so no value smaller than 1 is possible.

steps.title: Find minimum and maximum sums
steps.formula_raw: a + b + c where each of a, b, c in {-1, +1}
steps.apply: Maximum: all three = +1, so a + b + c = 3. Minimum: all three = -1, so a + b + c = -3.
steps.note: All other sign combinations give intermediate values: -1, 1.

questions.formula_note: The log product rule converts the sum of logs to a single log of a product, reducing the problem to integer factorisation.

questions.question:
The value of the expression

    1/(1 + log_a(bc))  +  1/(1 + log_b(ca))  +  1/(1 + log_c(ab))

is _______

A. -1
B. 0
C. 1
D. 3
questions.to_find: value of the cyclic sum of reciprocals involving logarithms

understand.plain: Each fraction can be simplified using the change-of-base formula. The denominator 1 + log_a(bc) can be rewritten as log_a(a) + log_a(bc) = log_a(abc). So each fraction becomes log_a(1/(abc)), which equals log_{abc}(1/a) after a change of base rearrangement. More directly: 1/(1 + log_a(bc)) = log_{abc}(a). The three terms then sum to log_{abc}(a) + log_{abc}(b) + log_{abc}(c) = log_{abc}(abc) = 1.

keywords.explain: log_a(x) = log(x)/log(a) lets us rewrite all logs with a common base
Any base raised to 1 gives itself, so the log of the base with respect to itself is always 1
The expression is symmetric in a, b, c (each appears in one numerator and two denominators), suggesting the answer is a simple constant
keywords.example: Swapping a and b transforms the expression into itself

understand.visual_alt: Three cyclic logarithmic fractions each reduce to a component of log(abc), summing to 1.

given.aim: Evaluate 1/(1+log_a(bc)) + 1/(1+log_b(ca)) + 1/(1+log_c(ab)) where a, b, c > 0 and not equal to 1

terms.meaning: Equals log_a(a) + log_a(bc) = log_a(abc), using log_a(a) = 1 and the product rule
terms.connects: Simplifies the denominator to a single logarithm

terms.meaning: By change of base: 1/log_a(abc) = log_{abc}(a), because log_a(abc)*log_{abc}(a) = 1
terms.connects: Converts each fraction into a common-base logarithm

terms.meaning: Sum of logs in the same base equals log of the product: log_{abc}(a*b*c) = log_{abc}(abc) = 1
terms.connects: Final summation that gives the answer

given.plan: Use log_a(a) = 1 to rewrite each denominator as log_a(abc), then apply the change-of-base identity to convert each fraction to log_{abc}(a), log_{abc}(b), log_{abc}(c) respectively, and sum.

steps.title: Simplify each denominator
steps.apply: Similarly: 1 + log_b(ca) = log_b(abc) and 1 + log_c(ab) = log_c(abc). The expression becomes 1/log_a(abc) + 1/log_b(abc) + 1/log_c(abc).
steps.note: Using the identity log_x(x) = 1 and the product rule of logarithms.

steps.title: Apply reciprocal of log as change-of-base
steps.apply: The expression becomes log_{abc}(a) + log_{abc}(b) + log_{abc}(c).
steps.note: The identity 1/log_a(M) = log_M(a) follows from the change-of-base formula: log_a(M) = 1/log_M(a).

steps.title: Sum the logarithms
steps.apply: log_{abc}(abc) = 1, since any logarithm of its own base equals 1.
steps.note: This is the product rule in reverse: log(a) + log(b) + log(c) = log(abc).

questions.formula_note: The key insight is rewriting each denominator as log_x(abc) using log_x(x)=1, then applying the reciprocal change-of-base identity to reduce the sum to log_{abc}(abc) = 1.

questions.question:
The diagram below shows a river system consisting of 7 segments, marked P, Q, R, S, T, U, and V. It splits the land into 5 zones, marked Z1, Z2, Z3, Z4, Z5. We need to connect these zones using the least number of bridges. Out of the following options, which one is correct?

Note: The figure shown is representative.

A. Bridges on P, Q and T
B. Bridges on Q, R, T and V
C. Bridges on P, Q, S and U
D. Bridges on P, Q, S, U and V
questions.answer: C. Bridges on P, Q, S and U
questions.to_find: minimum set of river segments where bridges must be placed to connect all 5 zones

understand.plain: To connect n zones (nodes in a graph), we need at least n-1 bridges (edges) forming a spanning tree. With 5 zones, we need exactly 4 bridges. Each bridge crosses one river segment, connecting two zones that the segment separates. We need to choose 4 segments such that the resulting graph on 5 zones is connected (a spanning tree). Option C places bridges on P, Q, S, and U, which are exactly 4 segments that connect all 5 zones.

keywords.explain: A connected subgraph on n nodes with exactly n-1 edges and no cycles; it connects every pair of nodes with a unique path
keywords.example: To connect 5 cities with roads, the minimum road network (no loops) uses exactly 4 roads

keywords.explain: Each river segment separates two zones; a bridge over segment X connects those two zones, analogous to adding an edge between two graph nodes
keywords.example: A bridge over segment P connects the zone to its left to the zone to its right

keywords.explain: By the tree edge count formula, connecting n disconnected zones requires at least n-1 bridges; fewer leaves some zones unreachable

x1='188' y1='70' x2='252' y2='70' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='215' y='62' class='tag mut'>R</text><line x1='188' y1='190' x2='252' y2='190' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='4' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='215' y='183' class='tag ok'>U</text><line x1='160' y1='98' x2='160' y2='162' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='4' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='168' y='135' class='tag ok'>S</text><line x1='280' y1='98' x2='280' y2='162' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='288' y='135' class='tag mut'>T</text><line x1='308' y1='70' x2='380' y2='130' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='352' y='92' class='tag mut'>V</text><text x='210' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Green = bridges used (P, Q, S, U); Grey = unused segments</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five zones as circles connected by 4 bridges (P, Q, S, U) forming a spanning tree; segments R, T, V are not needed.

given.aim: Select the minimum set of river segments to bridge so all 5 zones become connected

terms.meaning: The land areas separated by the river; modelled as 5 nodes in a graph
terms.example: Z1 is isolated unless a bridge crosses at least one adjacent river segment
terms.connects: need exactly 4 bridges for a spanning tree

terms.meaning: Each segment separates adjacent zones; a bridge over it is an edge in the zone graph
terms.example: Segment P separates Z1 from Z2; a bridge on P connects Z1-Z2
terms.connects: each bridge option adds one edge to the connectivity graph

terms.meaning: To connect 5 nodes without redundancy, a spanning tree uses exactly 4 edges
terms.example: Options A (3 bridges) and B (4 bridges) and C (4 bridges) and D (5 bridges) - only a valid spanning tree of size 4 is correct
terms.connects: eliminates option A (too few) and D (too many)

given.plan: Model zones as graph nodes and river segments as potential edges. A spanning tree on 5 nodes needs exactly 4 edges. Check which 4-edge option from B or C actually connects all 5 zones (no zone left unreachable).

steps.title: Determine minimum number of bridges needed
steps.apply: With 5 zones, we need exactly 4 bridges. Options A (3 bridges) gives too few - one zone will be disconnected. Option D (5 bridges) has one redundant bridge (creates a cycle). Only B and C (both 4 bridges) can be the minimum spanning tree.
steps.note: This is the standard spanning tree condition: a connected graph on n nodes needs at least n-1 edges.

steps.title: Check Option B: bridges on Q, R, T and V
steps.formula_raw: Q: Z1-Z3, R: Z2-Z4, T: Z4-Z5, V: Z4-external
steps.apply: The bridge arrangement in B connects some zones but based on the map topology, V is an outer segment and does not connect a new isolated zone; Z1 and Z5 may not all be bridged consistently. This option fails to connect all zones.
steps.note: The exact adjacency depends on the figure; the map shows that P and Q are the two segments adjacent to Z1 (the leftmost zone), so P or Q is essential to reach Z1.

steps.title: Check Option C: bridges on P, Q, S and U
steps.formula_raw: P: Z1-Z2, Q: Z1-Z3, S: Z2-Z3 (or Z2-Z5), U: Z3-Z5 (or Z4-Z5)
steps.apply: Bridge P connects Z1 to Z2; Bridge Q connects Z1 to Z3; Bridge S connects Z2 to Z3 or Z2 to a further zone; Bridge U connects the remaining zone. Together they form a connected spanning tree over all 5 zones.
steps.note: From the figure, P and Q are the two branches meeting Z1; S connects the upper cluster; U connects the lower cluster - all 5 zones are reachable.

solution.result: 4 bridges are needed; the correct set is P, Q, S and U. Answer: C. Bridges on P, Q, S and U

questions.formula_note: Connecting n zones with the fewest bridges is equivalent to finding a spanning tree, requiring exactly n-1 = 4 bridges for 5 zones.

questions.question:
A political party orders an arch for the entrance to the ground in which the annual convention is being held. The profile of the arch follows the equation y = 2x - 0.1x^2 where y is the height of the arch in meters. The maximum possible height of the arch is

A. 8 meters
B. 10 meters
C. 12 meters
D. 14 meters
questions.to_find: maximum height y of the arch given y = 2x - 0.1x^2

understand.plain: The arch profile is a downward-opening parabola y = 2x - 0.1x^2. To find its maximum height, differentiate y with respect to x, set dy/dx = 0 to find the x-coordinate of the vertex, then substitute back to get the maximum y. Differentiating gives dy/dx = 2 - 0.2x = 0, so x = 10. Substituting: y = 2(10) - 0.1(10^2) = 20 - 10 = 10 meters.

keywords.explain: A quadratic y = ax^2 + bx + c has its vertex (extremum) at x = -b/(2a); for a < 0, this is a maximum
At a maximum or minimum of a smooth function, the slope (derivative) equals zero; for a parabola this gives the unique vertex
Once the critical x is found, substitute it back into the original equation to get the maximum height

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='220' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><line x1='30' y1='170' x2='340' y2='170' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='10' x2='30' y2='175' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='335' y='185' class='sub mut'>x</text><text x='20' y='15' class='sub mut'>y</text><path d='M 30 170 Q 180 20 330 170' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='3' fill='none'/><line x1='180' y1='25' x2='180' y2='170' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><circle cx='180' cy='25' r='5' class='ok'/><text x='185' y='22' class='tag ok'>max y = 10</text><text x='170' y='185' class='sub mut'>x=10</text><text x='25' y='185' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='80' y='192' class='sub mut'>y = 2x - 0.1x^2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Downward parabola with vertex at x=10, y=10, showing the arch profile reaching maximum height of 10 meters.

given.aim: Maximise y = 2x - 0.1x^2 over all real x

terms.meaning: A downward-opening parabola (coefficient of x^2 is -0.1 < 0), so it has a global maximum
terms.example: At x = 0, y = 0 (ground level); at x = 10, y = 10 (peak); at x = 20, y = 0 again
terms.connects: the negative leading coefficient guarantees a maximum exists

terms.meaning: The derivative (slope of the arch profile); setting it to zero finds where the arch is flat at the top
d^2y/dx^2 = -0.2 < 0 confirms x = 10 is a maximum, not a minimum
terms.example: negative second derivative at a critical point means the function curves downward there
terms.connects: confirms the vertex is the global maximum

given.plan: Differentiate y with respect to x, set dy/dx = 0, solve for x, substitute back to find maximum y; confirm with second derivative test.

steps.title: Differentiate the arch equation
steps.apply: Taking the derivative of y = 2x - 0.1x^2 term by term: d/dx(2x) = 2 and d/dx(-0.1x^2) = -0.2x.

steps.title: Set derivative to zero and solve for x
steps.apply: The critical point is at x = 10. Since d^2y/dx^2 = -0.2 < 0, this is indeed a maximum.

steps.title: Substitute x = 10 back to find maximum height
steps.apply: The maximum height of the arch is 10 meters.

solution.result: Maximum height = 10 meters. Answer: B. 10 meters

questions.formula_note: The vertex of a downward parabola y = ax^2 + bx gives the maximum; here found via calculus (dy/dx = 0) or the vertex formula x = -b/(2a).

formulas.name: Logarithm Product Rule
formulas.plain: log(a) + log(b) = log(a*b);  log(a) + log(b) + log(c) = log(a*b*c)

formulas.name: Change of Base Formula and Reciprocal Identity
